data.title: PKW: Don't Miss The Growth From This Value-Oriented Fund
data.description:
There are a number of reasons a company may elect to buy back its own stock. For some, share repurchases are an alternative to dividends as a way to return capital to shareholders. Buyback activity can help boost earnings per share metrics, as a smaller total share count can make EPS comparisons look better, even if actual earnings remain flat. Share repurchases can also provide some support for the company’s stock, offering some price stability when its transactions are made in the open market.

Most see repurchase activity as a positive, even though some investors may prefer that companies use the cash for research or capital expenditures. It is this activity that is at the core of the objective of the Invesco BuyBack Achievers ETF (PKW). While this exchange-traded fund has some characteristics commonly found in a deep value investment portfolio, its historical returns are much more in line with the overall market.

As so much of the market's focus is centered around growth stocks with a high P/E ratio and earnings that may or may not take place in the future, PKW can offer investors a unique way to diversify their holdings without giving up the prospect for strong gains. I think its track record through both up and down markets over the last two decades makes it a Buy.

ETF Description

Invesco describes its BuyBack Achievers ETF as follows:

The Invesco BuyBack Achievers™ ETF is based on the NASDAQ US BuyBack Achievers™ Index. The Fund will normally invest at least 90% of its total assets in common stocks that comprise the Index. The Index is designed to track the performance of companies that meet the requirements to be classified as BuyBack Achievers™. The NASDAQ US BuyBack Achievers Index is comprised of US securities issued by corporations that have effected a net reduction in shares outstanding of 5% or more in the trailing 12 months.

Both PKW and the index that it follows are reconstituted twice a year, once in February and again in August. They are rebalanced at the start of each quarter.

The number of stocks that meet the criteria for this $1.6 billion fund is a bit higher than I expected. As of January 15, Invesco claimed 209 holdings. The top 10 holdings make up 39.2% of the total $1.6 billion in assets under management. The top three—the Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS), Chevron Corporation (CVX), and Wells Fargo & Company (WFC)—all have a weight of over 5%. Goldman Sachs just announced its Q4 2025 earnings on January 15. As part of its report, GS stated that it had executed almost $12 billion in share buybacks in FY 2025 and still has an additional $32 billion remaining under its most recent board authorization. The company also topped analysts’ expectations for the final quarter by $2.25 per share, earning $14.01 per share.

Energy giant Chevron is set to report its earnings for FY 2025 on January 30. As part of its last quarterly report, the company stated that it had spent $9.2 billion on share repurchases in the first nine months of the year. Its stock is off to a strong start to the new year, up 8.5% since January 1st. A complete list of PKW’s top holdings is listed below.

It should not be too much of a surprise that financials hold the largest sector allocation in the fund. While technology stocks led all sectors in buybacks in Q3 2025, many have not met the 5% total threshold required by the benchmark for this fund. Their buybacks are often used to balance out stock-based compensation.

PKW is diversified across all 11 market sectors, with consumer cyclicals and health care stocks joining financials in the top three by allocation. The two energy stocks seen in the fund's top 10 holdings, Chevron and Marathon Petroleum Corporation (MPC), comprise most of the fund's 8.83% exposure to that sector. Real estate stocks barely make the list with a weight of under 1%, as shown below.

While PKW is not really marketed as a mid-cap ETF, that is the market cap sector that is most predominant in its holdings. The fund has just over half of its assets allocated to mid-cap stocks. The remaining balance is a relatively even split between large and small caps.

The Invesco BuyBack Achievers ETF is not a great match for income-oriented investors as the fund yields just under 1%. Distributions are made during the final week of each quarter. The payout has been somewhat inconsistent over the last decade, with five years of annual growth of 35% or more but also with three years of declines of at least 15%.

ETF Analysis and Past Performance

At first glance, the characteristics of PKW have all the makings of a deep value fund. As of December 31, the last date that Invesco has updated its data on its website, the fund had an average price-to-earnings ratio of 15.35. That is well below the P/E ratio for the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), which was just over 30 as of January 16. PKW also reported that its portfolio had a mean price-to-book ratio of 2.25. IVV is over twice that mark at 5.60.

When I first saw those numbers for PKW, warning signs for a value trap were flashing in my head. However, the fund’s forward P/E surprised me. With an expected PE of 12.83x, the data implies earnings growth of almost 20%.

The returns of the fund over the last decade have been equally impressive. In addition to its own benchmark index, Invesco provides a comparison between the returns of PKW and the S&P 500. The difference is surprisingly small. Over the last year, there has only been a four basis point gap. Over the last five years, PKW’s returns have been only 38 basis points away from the S&P 500 on an annual basis. Since the fund’s inception in 2006, the difference is only 29 basis points.

There are no other peer funds that follow the exact same investing method as PKW, but when the fund is compared to other value-oriented ETFs, its performance seems to be near the front of the pack in most years. The fund currently sports a five-star rating from Morningstar for its risk-adjusted returns. PKW has posted double-digit gains in three of the last four years, with the only exception being 2022. During a 10-year period between 2016 and 2025, the fund finished in the top quartile of its category on Morningstar in seven of those years. In both 2024 and 2025, the fund was in the top 10% of its category, mid-cap value.

Risks to Consider

While PKW has certainly enjoyed periods of success, the fund also has experienced a few years of significant underperformance in the last decade. In the ten-year period mentioned earlier, those three years when the fund was not in the top quartile, it was in the bottom. During the down year of 2022, the fund significantly underperformed its peers from its Morningstar category. The fund has experienced a bit of an “all or nothing” record of past returns. While it is too early to know what 2026 will bring, PKW has fallen behind its peers by a sizable margin in the first three weeks of this year, as Morningstar states it is in the 92nd percentile for returns.

The fund’s sector weighting shows that it will struggle to post strong results when the financial sector is under pressure. Last year was a good one for banks and other financial services companies as the Fed lowered short-term rates, and many companies benefited from a healthy M&A environment. If unemployment rises in 2026, or if businesses lose confidence in their prospects for the future, the stocks in this sector could underperform. A recession or a significant slowdown in growth could lead to stress for bank loan portfolios and a rise in loan defaults. When compared to the S&P 500, PKW is also overweight consumer cyclical stocks, which could struggle if consumers cut back on their discretionary spending.

PKW has an expense ratio of 0.62%, which is above the median for all ETFs and significantly higher than what I would expect to see in a passively managed domestic stock fund. Over time, higher fees erode investor returns. This fund has been able to post strong results, as referenced above, and I am not opposed to paying higher fees when they are justified by results.

The Bottom Line

While all of the data has not been submitted for 2025, it looks like the year will set a new record for stock buybacks at approximately $1.2 trillion. This follows 2024, when the previous record of $942 billion was established. More and more companies are choosing to support their stocks in the open market with share repurchases, which may open up even more stocks eligible for consideration in PKW’s index in the year ahead.

The portfolio selection method followed by the Invesco BuyBack Achievers ETF has a solid track record of above-average returns. When I see a value-oriented fund that has been able to keep pace with the S&P 500 for an extended period of time, it deserves more consideration. I consider PKW to be a fund worth buying for investors who are looking for alternatives to standard index ETFs that may be exposed to portfolios that contain stocks with higher valuations.

data.title: Accenture: I'm Starting A Position (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Accenture (ACN) is an IT Consulting company in the Information Technology sector of the economy. In this article I will outline why I believe a position in ACN is warranted at this time. I will review its fundamentals as well as its technical outlook using my analysis of price action, momentum, volume, and relative strength. I last wrote about ACN back in 2018, and I recommended buying the stock at that time.

Table 1 - Seeking Alpha Factor Grades

Looking at Table 1, you can get an overview of how ACN is assessed. The valuation grade is neutral. Not great and not terrible. Growth is terrible. Not only is it terrible, but it's worse now than it was six months ago. Profitability is outstanding. It shows that the company prints money. Momentum has improved a great deal, and I will touch upon that later in the article. The revisions grade has deteriorated. It was much higher six months ago.

Table 2 - Upcoming Earnings

The upcoming quarter's earnings show how over the last 90 days, Wall Street analysts have revised their earnings projections to be lower for the most part. Sixteen analysts have revised their estimates lower, while only two analysts have revised their earnings estimates higher. I find this number striking, as this number seems to contradict the volume data that I will comment on later in the article.

While I am not a fundamental analyst by any measure, I side with price and volume. I believe that the earnings and revenues will come in above expectations. I say that because Chart 1 below shows bullish buying activity. Institutional money managers only buy stocks that they think are undervalued. For that reason, I believe that the next earnings report will surprise to the upside.

Now I will provide a technical analysis of ACN using my approach of analyzing price action, momentum, volume, and relative strength.

Chart 1 - CAN Weekly with 30-week EMA, Momentum, Volume, and Relative Strength

My approach to trading is that of a trend follower. I want to own stocks that are in bullish uptrends. I don't expect to buy at the absolute bottom nor sell at the absolute top, but I aim to capture a big portion of the move between the top and the bottom.

A filter I use to gauge trends is the 30-week exponential moving average (EMA). If the stock is trading above that EMA, I consider that stock to be in a bullish uptrend. Looking at ACN in Chart 1, it is trading above the 30-week EMA. The 30-week EMA is also starting to slope higher, which is bullish. Another indication of bullish price action is that ACN is now trading at its highest level in the past six months.

The orange rectangle on Chart 1 shows the 52-week high at the $390 level. This is the most recent high and a level of resistance where price couldn't get past. While not a prediction, the $390 level is a reasonable price target if ACN can get past the $320 level, which also acted as a level of resistance. The $390 level represents a 36% gain from the current price.

My next tool in my technical analysis toolkit is the Percentage Price Oscillator (PPO), which I use to measure momentum. I want to buy stocks that have bullish long-term momentum.

Looking at the top pane in Chart 1, when the black PPO line is above the red signal line, it shows bullish short-term momentum. That is the condition that ACN has now. More important to me is the long-term bullish momentum. When the black PPO line has a positive reading, a reading above zero, that is an indication of bullish long-term momentum. The black PPO line reads 0.110. It just came into long-term bullish territory last week. This reading coincides with the momentum grade improvement shown in Table 1 above. The last time the black PPO line went from under zero to over zero was late August 2024. After that, the stock rallied to $390.

I have found that a stock that trades above its 30-week EMA while having a bullish long-term momentum reading makes for excellent trades. This combination of events isn't guaranteed to work all the time but does work often in my experience.

Volume is the next technical element I consider when analyzing a stock to buy. I want to buy stocks that are being bought by institutional money managers. They only buy stocks that they consider to be undervalued.

The volume pane on Chart 1 shows red and black volume bars. The red volume bars show the number of shares traded for the week when the stock closed lower for the week. The black volume bars show the number of shares traded for the week when the stock closed higher for the week. I want to see more black bars than red bars, and I want to see the black bars peaking above the red bars.

The volume picture looks bullish, especially over the last three months. As the stock has rallied off the recent lows at $230, the black bars have been more prevalent, and the black bars have been higher on average than the red bars. That shows that institutions have been buying shares of ACN. They must believe that ACN is undervalued at current levels.

This volume picture does contradict the negative earnings revisions that I showed in Tables 1 and 2 above. The volume pattern shows accumulation, meaning the stock should have bullish earnings in the future. The revisions say that the future earnings will be lower than previously expected. I guess both things can be true at the same time. The earnings could be higher, not just as high as previously expected. As a technical trader, I believe the price is the right metric to follow. With that said, perhaps the next quarter's earnings may surprise to the upside. Only time will tell.

The last technical element I consider is relative strength. I want to own stocks that are outperforming the S&P 500 index. The relative strength line in the bottom pane of Chart 1 is easy to read. When the black line is trending higher, that means that ACN is outperforming the SP 500 index. Conversely, when the black line is trending lower, as it was from February 2025 to October 2025, that means that ACN was underperforming the major index.

Right now, the relative strength of ACN is about even with the S&P 500. The relative strength line is slightly higher now than it was at the lows back in September. I've seen more robust relative strength readings.

The technical picture looks bullish. Price is above its 30-week EMA, and the EMA is starting to slope upwards. Momentum is bullish, both short- and long-term. The volume picture looks bullish, and relative strength is neutral to slightly bullish. A potential price target of $390 is possible.

However, my analysis may be incorrect. Earnings could come in well below expectations, and those downward revisions would be spot on. And then there is the mid-term election issue. Markets historically don't perform well during mid-term election years. I touched upon this in this article last month. Due to these two factors alone, it is good to have a risk management plan. I would sell my position if ACN closes below the 30-week EMA. That would signal a potential change in trend, and I would gladly take a small loss and look for other opportunities rather than stay in a prolonged downtrend like the one seen from March 2025 to October 2025.

data.title: Cedar Creek Partners Q4 2025 Expert Market Exposure
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from this fund letter.

Our exposure to stocks trading in the expert market decreased to 36% of the fund. As a reminder, expert market stocks are companies impacted by SEC Rule 15c2-11. For those unfamiliar, the rule prevents brokers from not only displaying quotes for non-reporting companies but also restricts transactions for retail customers to selling only. 3 Institutional accounts, depending on the broker, are not subject to the buying restriction.

There are a wide range of differences among “non-reporting” companies. Some are completely dark – meaning they do not communicate any financial results to shareholders (unless forced to under law). Others update quarterly or annually on their website, sometimes behind a password login. Others mail annual results out to shareholders, or just shareholders of record, but do not post them publicly.

We were forced out of our position in Propel Media (PROM) (expert: PROM) at $1.451 per share. Five positions make up about 75% of the fund’s expert market exposure – Exco Resources (EXCE) (expert: EXCO) is 8.4% of the fund, Phi Group (PHIG) (expert: PHIG) is 8% of the fund, First IC (FIEB) (expert: FIEB) is 6%, PD-Rx Pharmaceuticals (PDRX) (expert: PDRX) is 3% and Federal Life Group (FLFG) (expert: FLFG) is 2.4% of the fund.

As we noted above, we were forced out of our position in Propel Media when it agreed to be purchased by Vitruvian Partners for $1.451 per share. If you haven’t read our Q2 2023 letter that discussed how we found Propel we encourage you to. During the quarter we received the proceeds. There is a possibility of an additional payment due to some funds being held in escrow. The overall return on the investment, including dividends, during our 28 month holding period, was over 500%. We had actually expected more. (I’m sure we will find a way to live with the disappointment of only earning 500%). The deal apparently was structured to include large bonus payments to management and included adjustments related to debt and working capital that took the topline number down significantly.

As we noted in our last two letters, First IC had agreed to be acquired by MetroCity Bankshares (MCBS) (nasdaq: MCBS) for cash and stock. The deal closed in early December; however, we are still waiting on both the cash and stock proceeds. We are told the delay is due to the shares we purchased having been from certificate sellers and the matching/reconciliation process is manual. It should be completed in the next week or so. Our basis on First IC was $6.70 per share. With purchases mostly in 2023. We received two $1 per share dividends and expected proceeds are roughly $22 per share. We are very pleased with the outcome, even if the selling price of less than ten times earnings seemed a bit light to us.

During the quarter we continued to increase our position in PHI Group (expert: PHIG). As we noted above, it is nearly an 8% position. We think it is worth two to three times the most recent trading price. PHI Group is a leading provider of helicopter flight services for the global oil and gas exploration and production industry and the air medical industry. The Company emerged from bankruptcy protection in September 2019. The bankruptcy was primarily due to excessive debt rather than poor financial performance. PHI has subsequently focused on improving its margins and prioritizing a conservative balance sheet. The Company had filed an S-1 registration statement but withdrew it in May of this year. Oaktree and First Pacific Advisors each were listed as owning approximately 18% of the common shares.

Federal Life Group (expert: FLFG) announced that its 90% owned parent, which we believe is Bain (BCSF), was forcing out shareholders via a short-form merger at $15.25 per share. We had been happily building a position at $9 to $9.50 per share and are disappointed that we cannot continue to do so. The company did not report publicly or privately; however, its primary subsidiary was required to report results to insurance regulators. The subsidiary was showing impressive growth and earnings while still investing heavily in the business and in a Bermuda-based reinsurer. In the first nine months of 2025 their Federal Life Insurance Co. subsidiary reported over $20 million in profits versus just $8 million in the first nine months of 2024.

During the quarter we added a new major expert market position, Exco Resources (expert: EXCE). We have followed the company for a number of years and previously owned shares in 2021. Exco is an independent oil and gas company focused on onshore US shale development and production. Their primary areas of focus are Haynesville and Bossier shale in east Texas and north Louisiana, Eagle Ford shale in south Texas, and Marcellus and Utica shales in Appalachia. Exco went through a restructuring and emerged from Chapter 11 in 2019. Exco has performed very well since then (see stock chart to the right). Production is nearly 90% natural gas.

At the end of 2020 Exco had just over $560 million of equity ($11 per share based on 51 million shares). Fast forward to September 2025 and Exco had equity of nearly $1.1 billion ($23 per share based on roughly 46 million shares). Mark-to-market gains and losses on derivatives (hedging) make their earnings swing wildly. We ignore most of that and focus on operating income and cash flow. What caught our attention was their increased production the last three quarters - from 22 Bcfe (billion cubic feet equivalent) in the March quarter, rising to 27 Bcfe in June, and then to nearly 36 Bcfe in September. Additionally, in December they announced a $430 million capex budget for 2026 compared to a ~$185 million budget for 2025.

We don't think you have to be an expert on future natural gas prices or well flow rates to like the setup. With production set to increase and having a proven track record of hedging successfully, we are quite optimistic. If natural gas goes to $2 per mcf, Exco is near breakeven, but will have about $4 per share in cash flow. Assuming a 10% increase in production and pricing of $3 per mcf we think they can earn about $3 per share (and have $7 per share in cash flow), and at $4 per mcf they would push $6 per share (and have total cash flow of about $10 per share) in comparison to its current $19 per share price. As we write this, current two year strip pricing averages about $3.75 per mcf. Hopefully we are underestimating their potential production increase.

Lastly as of the end of 2024 reserves were 28 times production. Admittedly, 75% of reserves were undeveloped, but that is still quite impressive. Their PV-10, which is the estimated present value of Exco's future after tax cash flows discounted back at a 10% rate, was nearly $40 per share. Natural gas pricing outlook is more favorable today than it was at the end of 2024. Obviously, they would be an attractive acquisition candidate.

Footnotes

1 While, no single index is directly comparable to Cedar Creek Partners, we believe that it is important to compare our performance to a passively managed approach. At the core of our investment philosophy is the belief that we can generate superior risk-adjusted returns by holding a more concentrated portfolio of under-valued securities, than an index holding a far greater number of securities. Index returns are calculated from information reported on Yahoo! Finance, Dow Jones, and Russell (see Disclaimer for more information).

2 Ratio excludes cash held by the fund. We add back non-economic amortization in our earnings estimate. Due to the uncertainty of the situation, we did not attribute any earnings or book value for Pacific Coast Oil Trust (ROYTL). We did not include excess capital of our ECIP bank holdings as excess cash, but did for the small mutual bank conversions we own.

3 We are aware of one major discount broker that still displays quotes to its customers, most do not even provide that making selling difficult. There are no public quotations such as on otcmarkets.com .

Disclaimers

Fund Performance

The financial performance figures for 2025 presented in this report are un-audited estimates based on the best information available at the time of the letter and are subject to subsequent revision by the Fund's auditors.Past performance may not be indicative of future results and no representation is made that an investor will or is likely to achieve results similar to those shown. All investments involve risk including the loss of principal.

Net Return reflects the experience of an investor who came into the Fund on inception and did not add to or withdraw from the Fund through the end of the most recently reported period. The reported net return figures will therefore include the impact of high water marks in the cumulative return. Individual investor returns will vary depending upon the timing of their investment, the effects of additions and withdrawals from their capital account, and each individual's high water mark figure, if any.

Index Returns

The S&P500 (SPX) Index returns are reported using the S&P500 Depository Receipt Trust (SPDR) which trades under the ticker symbol SPY. Reinvested dividends are included in these figures. A spreadsheet showing the SPY performance versus the fund since inception is available upon request.

Nasdaq Composite (IXIC) performance excludes dividends, which historically have been immaterial to the total return of that index. In recent years more technology stocks have begun paying dividends thus the inclusion of dividends would increase the reported figures.

Russell 2000 (RUT) performance is from data reported on Russell's website and includes reinvested dividends.

DJIA (DJI) returns are reported using the SPDR Dow Jones Industrial Average (DIA) which trades under the ticker symbol DIA. Reinvested dividends are included in these figures. A spreadsheet showing the DIA performance versus the fund since inception is available upon request.

While reported returns for SPY and DIA will likely be a few tenths of a percentage lower than the representative index annually, we believe they are a better reflection of what a non-institutional investor would earn following a passive investment approach.

Index returns are provided as a convenience to the reader only. The Fund's returns are likely to differ substantially from that of any index, and there can be no assurance that the Fund will achieve results that are superior to such indices.

Share Prices

Share price figures for listed stocks are from Yahoo! Finance and unless specified otherwise are the closing price as of the previous month end. Share price figures for unlisted stocks are closing bid prices as reported on otcmarkets.com, except for unlisted stocks classified as expert market, which do not have public availability of quotes, and are marked to last sale.

Forward Looking Statements

This letter and the accompanying discussion include forward-looking statements.

All statements that are not historical facts are forward-looking statements, including any statements that relate to future market conditions, results, operations, strategies or other future conditions or developments and any statements regarding objectives, opportunities, positioning or prospects. Forward-looking statements are necessarily based upon speculation, expectations, estimates and assumptions that are inherently unreliable and subject to significant business, economic and competitive uncertainties and contingencies. Forward-looking statements are not a promise or guaranty about future events.

data.title: Xenia Hotels & Resorts: Solid Fundamentals And Rising Dividends
data.description:
Introduction

I have written about Xenia Hotels & Resorts (XHR) a couple of times previously, most recently in April 2025, almost 9 months ago. Back then, I rated the company as a Hold, largely due to its low dividend yield. With the company scheduled to release its Q4 2025 earnings next month, now seems like a good time for me to re-evaluate the company to see if my rating remains the same.

Business Model

Xenia Hotels & Resorts has a portfolio of 30 luxury and upper-upscale hotels comprising 8,868 rooms, spread across 22 markets in 14 states. Its hotels are geographically well-diversified. While its top five markets – Houston, Orlando, San Diego, Atlanta and Nashville – collectively generate more than half (56%) of the company’s EBITDA, this is mitigated by the fact that no single market accounts for more than 17% of its EBITDA. In fact, only 2 markets (Orlando and Houston) account for over 10% of the company’s EBITDA.

Unlike leisure-oriented hotel REITs, Xenia Hotels & Resorts derives the bulk of its revenue from business transient (40%) and group (35%) segments, with the remaining (25%) coming from the leisure transient segment. This diversification enables the company to smooth out performance through the ups and downs of the travel market.

Balance Sheet

As of 30 September 2025, Xenia Hotels & Resorts has a total debt of $1.44 billion with a weighted average interest rate of 5.63% and a maturity profile providing flexibility to management. Firstly, its debt is spread out well, with little debt due this year and the next ($52 million in 2026 and $104 million in 2027). Next, 77% of the debt is either fixed or hedged, which helps reduce interest rate risk to the company.

The company has a net debt to adjusted EBITDAre of approximately 5x, though this is mitigated by several factors. First, the company has a substantial liquidity position of $688 million, including $500 million from its undrawn line of credit, which would be more than sufficient to handle short-term operating needs. Also, the bulk of its portfolio (27 of its 30 hotels) are unencumbered. This provides additional flexibility in the form of collateral should the company see the need to use it.

Dividends

The company has been increasing its quarterly dividends over the past 3 years. In 2023, the company was paying out a quarterly dividend of $0.10/share. This then increased to $0.12/share in 2024 and finally the current $0.14/share in 2025. Based off the current share price of $14.42, this gives the company a dividend of around 3.88%.

In the first 3 quarters of 2025, the company reported adjusted funds from operations (AFFO) of $1.31/share, up 9.2% compared to the same period in 2024. The company has also increased its guidance for the FY 2025, up from between $1.55/share and $1.74/share to between $1.68/share and $1.76/share. Even looking at the low end of guidance, this gives the company a dividend payout ratio of just 33%, suggesting the company’s current dividend is more than secure.

While the company’s dividend is nothing to boast about at the moment, there are a couple of factors that lead me to think the dividend may increase again in the near future. First, the company has been consistently increasing its dividends each year, and it is likely it will do so again for 2026. Next, management has previously indicated in earlier earnings calls that it expects its dividend payout ratio to return to pre-pandemic levels in the mid-60% range, almost double the current dividend payout ratio. While it would be far-fetched to expect the dividend to double immediately, I believe it is safe to assume that we can expect to see gradual increases until management reaches its target.

Valuation

In 2025, Xenia Hotels & Resorts has been fairly aggressive in deploying its capital. In the first 3 quarters of the year, the company spent $83.8 million to repurchase 6,656,706 shares at a weighted average price of $12.59/share. Moreover, the company still has an additional $134.1 million available for share repurchases. In fact, in its Q3 2025 earnings call, management highlighted how the company would likely allocate capital to buybacks instead of adding to its hotel portfolio, as they “continue to look probably more attractive than acquisitions at this point.”

By choosing to allocate capital towards share buybacks instead of other acquisitions or even reducing its debt, management has clearly indicated its conviction that the company’s shares are trading below their intrinsic value. This is also supported by analyst ratings, which have an average price target of $16.40/share, a potential 13% upside. This indicates that the stock is, at present, likely to be undervalued.

Risks

As always, there are potential risks to consider. First, the cyclical nature of the hospitality industry means the company is sensitive to broader economic fluctuations and seasonal changes. This is especially important as the luxury sector would be disproportionately affected by any recession.

Next, I do have some concerns over the company’s decision to focus entirely on buybacks. While the company has no doubt performed well over the years despite its lack of acquisitions, there is the worry that this will cause the company to miss out on buying opportunities.

Additionally, not using the additional cash to pay off its debt and reduce leverage may come back to haunt the company if a recession happens. Nevertheless, management seems to have made the correct decision thus far, as seen by the close to 40% increase in share price since my last article.

Conclusion

In my previous article on the company, I rated it as a Hold mainly due to its low dividend yield. Since then, the dividend yield has fallen, though this has more to do with the increase in share price rather than a decrease in the actual payout. The company’s balance sheet remains healthy, and shares seem undervalued at present. Moreover, the dividend payout ratio is very comfortable, and all things point to another increase this year. As such, I rate the company as a Buy.

data.title: Cedar Creek Partners Q4 2025 Letter
data.description:
Dear Partners and Friends:

2025 was another strong year for US stock markets. All the major indices we compare against rose double digits. Micro cap stocks outperformed for the first time in a while. The Russell Microcap (IWC) index rose 23.0%, even though the Russell 2000 (IWM) only rose 12.8%. The NASDAQ Composite (^IXIC) once again was the best performer of the three major indices, as it climbed 20.4%. The S&P500 (SPY (SPY)) rose 17.8% on the year. Cedar Creek was up 29.6%, net of fees and expenses, outperforming all the major indices we track. 1

While Cedar Creek Partners focuses primarily on microcap stocks, and over-the-counter stocks in particular, we compare our returns against larger indices as well since we believe we need to outperform the most prominent passive benchmarks over time in order to justify our existence.

Cedar Creek's average annual return over our nearly 20 year history is 14.74%, net of fees and expenses, which compares favorably to all the indices we compare against. Cumulative returns since inception for Cedar Creek were 1,455.1%, net of fees and expenses.

Fund Holdings are at Incredibly Attractive Prices

On the whole, as of the end of December 2025, the fund's holdings were trading at 7.1 times our estimate of earnings for the coming year, and 5.2 times expected earnings net of cash at the respective businesses. 2 Trailing earnings multiple was 9.6 times as of December 2025. Weighted price-to-book was 0.9. Dividend yield was 1.8% as of December 2025. Weighted expected return on equity as of December 31, 2025, was 13.0%. (note – in most of the fund’s investments, we are earnings focused and not book value or dividend yield focused.)

Cash Levels and Fund Repositioning

We started the quarter with cash levels at 17%, which was unusually high due to the sale of PharmChem in the third quarter, and ended the quarter at 13%. It is currently 7% and we have a verbal purchase agreement that will use half of it. We are still waiting on proceeds from the sale of First IC (expert: FIEB), which will move cash back to the 6-7% range. We currently want to keep roughly 5% for any redemptions and dry powder should something attractive arise. We definitely still have more ideas than cash.

Expert Market Exposure

Our exposure to stocks trading in the expert market decreased to 36% of the fund. As a reminder, expert market stocks are companies impacted by SEC Rule 15c2-11. For those unfamiliar, the rule prevents brokers from not only displaying quotes for non-reporting companies but also restricts transactions for retail customers to selling only. 3 Institutional accounts, depending on the broker, are not subject to the buying restriction.

There are a wide range of differences among “non-reporting” companies. Some are completely dark – meaning they do not communicate any financial results to shareholders (unless forced to under law). Others update quarterly or annually on their website, sometimes behind a password login. Others mail annual results out to shareholders, or just shareholders of record, but do not post them publicly.

We were forced out of our position in Propel Media (PROM) (expert: PROM) at $1.451 per share. Five positions make up about 75% of the fund’s expert market exposure – Exco Resources (EXCE) (expert: EXCO) is 8.4% of the fund, Phi Group (PHIG) (expert: PHIG) is 8% of the fund, First IC (FIEB) (expert: FIEB) is 6%, PD-Rx Pharmaceuticals (PDRX) (expert: PDRX) is 3% and Federal Life Group (FLFG) (expert: FLFG) is 2.4% of the fund.

As we noted above, we were forced out of our position in Propel Media when it agreed to be purchased by Vitruvian Partners for $1.451 per share. If you haven’t read our Q2 2023 letter that discussed how we found Propel we encourage you to. During the quarter we received the proceeds. There is a possibility of an additional payment due to some funds being held in escrow. The overall return on the investment, including dividends, during our 28 month holding period, was over 500%. We had actually expected more. (I’m sure we will find a way to live with the disappointment of only earning 500%). The deal apparently was structured to include large bonus payments to management and included adjustments related to debt and working capital that took the topline number down significantly.

As we noted in our last two letters, First IC had agreed to be acquired by MetroCity Bankshares (MCBS) (nasdaq: MCBS) for cash and stock. The deal closed in early December; however, we are still waiting on both the cash and stock proceeds. We are told the delay is due to the shares we purchased having been from certificate sellers and the matching/reconciliation process is manual. It should be completed in the next week or so. Our basis on First IC was $6.70 per share. With purchases mostly in 2023. We received two $1 per share dividends and expected proceeds are roughly $22 per share. We are very pleased with the outcome, even if the selling price of less than ten times earnings seemed a bit light to us.

During the quarter we continued to increase our position in PHI Group (expert: PHIG). As we noted above, it is nearly an 8% position. We think it is worth two to three times the most recent trading price. PHI Group is a leading provider of helicopter flight services for the global oil and gas exploration and production industry and the air medical industry. The Company emerged from bankruptcy protection in September 2019. The bankruptcy was primarily due to excessive debt rather than poor financial performance. PHI has subsequently focused on improving its margins and prioritizing a conservative balance sheet. The Company had filed an S-1 registration statement but withdrew it in May of this year. Oaktree and First Pacific Advisors each were listed as owning approximately 18% of the common shares.

Federal Life Group (expert: FLFG) announced that its 90% owned parent, which we believe is Bain, was forcing out shareholders via a short-form merger at $15.25 per share. We had been happily building a position at $9 to $9.50 per share and are disappointed that we cannot continue to do so. The company did not report publicly or privately; however, its primary subsidiary was required to report results to insurance regulators. The subsidiary was showing impressive growth and earnings while still investing heavily in the business and in a Bermuda-based reinsurer. In the first nine months of 2025 their Federal Life Insurance Co. subsidiary reported over $20 million in profits versus just $8 million in the first nine months of 2024.

During the quarter we added a new major expert market position, Exco Resources (expert: EXCE). We have followed the company for a number of years and previously owned shares in 2021. Exco is an independent oil and gas company focused on onshore US shale development and production. Their primary areas of focus are Haynesville and Bossier shale in east Texas and north Louisiana, Eagle Ford shale in south Texas, and Marcellus and Utica shales in Appalachia. Exco went through a restructuring and emerged from Chapter 11 in 2019. Exco has performed very well since then (see stock chart to the right). Production is nearly 90% natural gas.

At the end of 2020 Exco had just over $560 million of equity ($11 per share based on 51 million shares). Fast forward to September 2025 and Exco had equity of nearly $1.1 billion ($23 per share based on roughly 46 million shares). Mark-to-market gains and losses on derivatives (hedging) make their earnings swing wildly. We ignore most of that and focus on operating income and cash flow. What caught our attention was their increased production the last three quarters - from 22 Bcfe (billion cubic feet equivalent) in the March quarter, rising to 27 Bcfe in June, and then to nearly 36 Bcfe in September. Additionally, in December they announced a $430 million capex budget for 2026 compared to a ~$185 million budget for 2025.

We don't think you have to be an expert on future natural gas prices or well flow rates to like the setup. With production set to increase and having a proven track record of hedging successfully, we are quite optimistic. If natural gas goes to $2 per mcf, Exco is near breakeven, but will have about $4 per share in cash flow. Assuming a 10% increase in production and pricing of $3 per mcf we think they can earn about $3 per share (and have $7 per share in cash flow), and at $4 per mcf they would push $6 per share (and have total cash flow of about $10 per share) in comparison to its current $19 per share price. As we write this, current two year strip pricing averages about $3.75 per mcf. Hopefully we are underestimating their potential production increase.

Lastly as of the end of 2024 reserves were 28 times production. Admittedly, 75% of reserves were undeveloped, but that is still quite impressive. Their PV-10, which is the estimated present value of Exco's future after tax cash flows discounted back at a 10% rate, was nearly $40 per share. Natural gas pricing outlook is more favorable today than it was at the end of 2024. Obviously, they would be an attractive acquisition candidate.

Performance Attribution

Last quarter we noted that we currently think of the fund as having three categories: expert market stocks, control positions (where the fund manager is on the board and can have greater influence over decision making), and generally undervalued securities.

Most of the fund's performance in the fourth quarter was due to the increase in the valuation of our control position. It increased 14% in the quarter. Our expert market stocks increased in value by just under 3% in the fourth quarter, while our generally undervalued securities increased less than 1%.

Update on Our Control Position

As we noted above, by control, we mean that the fund manager is on the Board or in a position to significantly influence decision making, not that he has total control and gets what he wants. Solitron (SODI) has, and PharmChem had, a strong independent board, which is the way it should be at all companies.

Solitron Devices (SODI) (otc: SODI) - the bid price for shares increased from $16.40 per share to $18.69 per share during the fourth quarter. As a reminder, the fund manager is CEO and a board member of Solitron. The fund owns 11.2% of Solitron's outstanding shares and the fund manager and his family own 3.0%.

Solitron's earnings have been disappointing the last year and a half due to lower revenues owing to the timing of receipt of orders. Solitron started seeing a material uptick in orders (bookings) beginning in the November 2024 quarter. That quarter included a large order for HIMARS (High Mobility Artillery Rocket System) components from L3Harris (LHX) - Solitron's second largest defense customer. In the fiscal fourth quarter ending February 28, 2025, bookings were $8.9 million, and included over $5 million from Solitron's largest defense customer, RTX (formerly Raytheon (RTX)) for AMRAAM (Advanced Medium-Range Air-to-Air Missile) components. The May and August quarters bookings exceeded sales despite no orders related to HIMARS or AMRAAM. Solitron's November 2025 bookings were its highest ever, at $13.9 million, which included the next AMRAAM order.

Due to being an insider we only restate what has been publicly released in our SEC filings or press releases. We also do not provide any valuation estimates for Solitron.

Update on Other Fund Holdings

ENDI Corp (ENDI) (otc: ENDI) – we profiled ENDI in our 2024 first quarter letter when the share price was around $6.50 per share. The share price declined during the fourth quarter from $17.55 per share to $16.75 per share. ENDI owns CrossingBridge Advisors (ENDI) which manages fixed income mutual funds and a few managed accounts. Assets under management (AUM) for CrossingBridge grew over 30% in 2024, from $2.6 billion at the beginning of 2024, adjusted for the acquisition of the RiverPark Short Term High Yield Fund, to $3.4 billion at the end of 2024. According to their website, as of the end of December 2025, AUM was in excess of $4.1 billion, an increase of over 19% in 2025, despite a modest decrease in Q4. While not a central component of our thesis, with short term rates falling, we think investors may move into the short-term bond category to gain higher yield than money markets.

ENDI's reported earnings and operating margins do not reflect what is happening at the company. The first issue is the accounting treatment for the change in warrant liability. The company issued warrants as part of the CrossingBridge acquisition. Under accounting rules, the change in value is run through the income statement, such that when the company's price increases they are hit with a charge, and vice versa. We think it is one of the dumber accounting rules since it makes earnings improve when their stock price decreases and decline when their stock price increases. Thankfully, right before yearend, ENDI reached an agreement for an early cashless exercise.

The second issue is amortization of purchased management contracts. ENDI purchased management contracts on some of the funds it advises for, which created intangible amortization. These are non-cash charges and only reflect reality if the value of the management contract was declining at the amortization rate, which is not the case. The value, in our opinion, is actually increasing. Regardless, it hurts operating income and reported earnings. The Company helps investors understand this by reporting adjusted operating margins and adjusted EBITDA. Adjusted operating margins are in excess of 50%, which is excellent.

A third issue, is the presence of non-controlling interest which is how accounting rules treat ENDI's decision to sell 25% of CrossingBridge in 2025 for $25.9 million. The sale involved cash coming to the company in exchange for what is essentially a revenue royalty to the minority investors (Cbe Llc). The fund, and other entities affiliated with the fund manager, including Solitron, participated in the transaction. We think it is a win-win deal. It shows the value of CrossingBridge and provides additional capital to good capital allocators to grow assets under management. We discussed that transaction in our second quarter letter .

The end result is that we have to make a number of accounting adjustments to arrive at what we think is a clearer picture of true economic reality for ENDI. After the warrant exercise, ENDI has approximately 6.65 million shares outstanding, resulting in a market cap of ~$105 million. Cash and investments were $55 million as of September 2025, and the only debt was a $10 million note. Adjusted EBITDA was $3.1 million in the September quarter, or $12.5 million annualized. We expect them to introduce some additional products in the next year which should help AUM growth to continue. Our fair value estimate keeps rising as the company continues to execute, which is what you ideally want in all your equity investments. We look forward to what 2026 will bring.

Community Banks

We noted in our last letter that the fund has been building up a basket of what it sees as attractively priced community banks. At year end, 20% of the fund was in bank stocks. This included First IC mentioned above, which was just over 6% of the fund, as well as a few ECIP banks we re-entered into in Q4 as prices fell, Skyline Bankshares (SLBK) (otc: SLBK) which we profiled in our Q1 2025 letter, and the basket of smaller banks we have been building. The basket is mainly modestly growing community banks trading at less than six times earnings, which we believe should trade at higher valuations, and would be attractive acquisition candidates.

While the ECIP banks have underperformed in the recent year or so, we think they will screen better once they can repurchase the outstanding preferred stock at a deep discount. Some will be trading at half of book value and around six times earnings. If they do not make acquisitions or improve their valuations via increased dividends or share repurchases, they will likely become targets for acquisition.

Room for New Members and/or Additional Funds

We continue to have more attractive ideas than capital. Thus, there is plenty of room for existing partners to increase their investment and for others to join. Please consider referring friends of yours who may be potential new investors. The basic requirements are 1) that each invests a minimum of $100,000 and 2) that new members are accredited (high net worth) individuals. Subsequent investments must be for a minimum of $10,000.

If this letter was passed on to you and you would like to be added to our monthly distribution list, please email me at the email address below. You can find more letters at eriksencapitalmgmt.com/investor-letters . Should you have any questions regarding the fund, please don't hesitate to call or email.

Sincerely,

Tim Eriksen, Manager, Cedar Creek Partners LLC

Footnotes

1 While, no single index is directly comparable to Cedar Creek Partners, we believe that it is important to compare our performance to a passively managed approach. At the core of our investment philosophy is the belief that we can generate superior risk-adjusted returns by holding a more concentrated portfolio of under-valued securities, than an index holding a far greater number of securities. Index returns are calculated from information reported on Yahoo! Finance, Dow Jones, and Russell (see Disclaimer for more information).

2 Ratio excludes cash held by the fund. We add back non-economic amortization in our earnings estimate. Due to the uncertainty of the situation, we did not attribute any earnings or book value for Pacific Coast Oil Trust (ROYTL). We did not include excess capital of our ECIP bank holdings as excess cash, but did for the small mutual bank conversions we own.

3 We are aware of one major discount broker that still displays quotes to its customers, most do not even provide that making selling difficult. There are no public quotations such as on otcmarkets.com .

Disclaimers

Fund Performance

The financial performance figures for 2025 presented in this report are un-audited estimates based on the best information available at the time of the letter and are subject to subsequent revision by the Fund's auditors.

Past performance may not be indicative of future results and no representation is made that an investor will or is likely to achieve results similar to those shown. All investments involve risk including the loss of principal.

Net Return reflects the experience of an investor who came into the Fund on inception and did not add to or withdraw from the Fund through the end of the most recently reported period. The reported net return figures will therefore include the impact of high water marks in the cumulative return. Individual investor returns will vary depending upon the timing of their investment, the effects of additions and withdrawals from their capital account, and each individual's high water mark figure, if any.

Index Returns

The S&P500 Index returns are reported using the S&P500 Depository Receipt Trust (SPDR) which trades under the ticker symbol SPY. Reinvested dividends are included in these figures. A spreadsheet showing the SPY performance versus the fund since inception is available upon request.

Nasdaq Composite performance excludes dividends, which historically have been immaterial to the total return of that index. In recent years more technology stocks have begun paying dividends thus the inclusion of dividends would increase the reported figures.

Russell 2000 performance is from data reported on Russell's website and includes reinvested dividends.

DJIA returns are reported using the SPDR Dow Jones Industrial Average which trades under the ticker symbol DIA. Reinvested dividends are included in these figures. A spreadsheet showing the DIA performance versus the fund since inception is available upon request.

While reported returns for SPY and DIA will likely be a few tenths of a percentage lower than the representative index annually, we believe they are a better reflection of what a non-institutional investor would earn following a passive investment approach.

Index returns are provided as a convenience to the reader only. The Fund's returns are likely to differ substantially from that of any index, and there can be no assurance that the Fund will achieve results that are superior to such indices.

Share Prices

Share price figures for listed stocks are from Yahoo! Finance and unless specified otherwise are the closing price as of the previous month end. Share price figures for unlisted stocks are closing bid prices as reported on otcmarkets.com, except for unlisted stocks classified as expert market, which do not have public availability of quotes, and are marked to last sale.

Fast Facts

Vanguard Industrials Index Fund ETF Shares (VIS) was launched on 9/23/2004 and tracks the MSCI US IMI Industrials 25/50 Index. It is also available as a mutual fund (VINAX). VIS has a portfolio of 389 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 1.02%, and an expense ratio of 0.09%. Distributions are paid quarterly. It is a large and liquid ETF, with $6.8 billion of AUM (assets under management) and an average daily dollar volume of $22.6 million.

Vanguard Group, founded in 1975, is based in Malvern, Pennsylvania, and has about $11 trillion in total AUM. Vanguard is owned by its U.S.-based funds, which are owned by their shareholders.

Strategy

As described by MSCI, the underlying index includes large, mid, and small-cap companies listed on U.S. exchanges and classified in the Industrials sector by the Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS®). This sector includes companies manufacturing and distributing capital goods and those offering construction, engineering, transportation, and commercial and professional services. The index is weighted based on adjusted market capitalization and rebalanced quarterly. The “25-50” diversification rule ensures that no single issuer weights more than 25% and all issuers weighting more than 5% don’t exceed 50% in aggregate. The fund’s turnover rate was 5% in the most recent fiscal year.

This article will use as a benchmark the Industrial Select Sector Index, represented by State Street® Industrial Select Sector SPDR® ETF (XLI).

Portfolio

About 45% of asset value is in large companies, 38% in mid-caps, and 17% in small and micro-caps. The portfolio has significant exposure in aerospace/defense (24%) and machinery (19.5%), while other industries are below 11%.

The portfolio is diversified, with moderate company-specific risk. The top 10 issuers, listed in the next table, represent 29.5% of asset value, and the largest position (General Electric) weighs 5.25%.

Takeaway: Who Should Consider VIS?

VIS is well-suited for long-term investors seeking capital-weighted exposure in the industrials sector. It may also fit in tactical and rotational strategies involving an array of sector ETFs. Investors seeking a balanced portfolio with very low company risk and better historical performance may prefer the equal-weighted ETF RSPN, despite its higher fees.

  • Pros: low fees, diversification, quality above historical baseline.
  • Cons: Overvalued based on historical averages, lags RSPN.

This article answers these three main questions about VIS:

  1. What kind of holdings does VIS have, and how is its portfolio structured?
  2. How does VIS compare to its benchmarks and peer ETFs?
  3. What type of strategy is VIS best suited for?

data.title: Pending Home Sales Sink -9.3% In December
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The National Association of Realtors (NAR) pending home sales index experienced its largest monthly loss since 2020, falling -9.3% to 71.8. This was significantly worse than the expected -0.3% decline and marks a -3.0% fall from one year ago.

“The housing sector is not out of the woods yet,” said NAR Chief Economist Lawrence Yun. “After several months of encouraging signs in pending contracts and closed sales, the December new contract figures have dampened the short-term outlook.”

“Even after accounting for typical seasonal patterns, interpreting in-person home search activity in the winter – especially in December – can be tricky due to public holidays, people taking time off, and wintry weather conditions,” Yun added. “We’ll be watching the data in the coming months to determine whether the soft contract signings were a one-month aberration or the start of an underlying trend.”

“Data shows closing activity increased in December. However, new listings did not keep pace so inventory decreased. Consumers prefer seeing abundant inventory before making the major decision of purchasing a home. So, the decline in pending home sales could be a result of dampened consumer enthusiasm about buying a home when there are so few options listed for sale. In December there were only 1.18 million homes on the market – matching the lowest inventory level of 2025.”

Read more

Pending Home Sales Background

The pending home sales index (PHSI) was created by the National Association of Realtors to track home sales where the contract is signed, but the transaction has not yet closed. An index of 100 is equal to the level of contract activity in 2001. The PHSI is a leading economic indicator of future existing home sales.

The chart below gives us a snapshot of the index since 2001, the first year data was analyzed.

Over this time frame, the US population has grown by 20.9%. For a better look at the underlying trend, here is an overlay with the nominal index and the population-adjusted variant. The focus is pending home sales growth since 2001.

The above chart shows the percent of turn-of-the-century values. The index for the most recent month is currently 44% below its all-time high from August 2020. The population-adjusted index is 51% off its high from April 2005.

Pending Home Sales vs. Existing Home Sales

Pending home sales measure the change in the number of homes under contract to be sold but still awaiting the closing transaction. Whereas existing home sales measure the number of home sales by mortgage closings.

The NAR explained that "because a home goes under contract a month or two before it is sold, the Pending Home Sales Index generally leads Existing-Home Sales by a month or two." The pending home sales index is considered a more relevant and accurate measure of the real estate market.

Here is a growth overlay of the two series. The general correlation, as expected, is close. A close look at the numbers supports the NAR's assessment that their pending sales series is a leading index. Since 2001, pending home sales have fallen 29.3% while existing home sales have fallen 14.7%.

The 30-Year Fixed Mortgage Rate

According to Freddie Mac, the average 30-year fixed rate mortgage in December 2025 was 6.19%, the lowest level in over a year. Here is a look back at the 30-year fixed-rate mortgage weekly average, beginning in April 1971.

ETFs associated with residential real-estate include the iShares Residential and Multisector Real Estate ETF (REZ).

Original Post
