data.title: Pizza Pizza Royalty: A Piece Of Advice - Buy It
data.description:
We have seen some interesting changes in the months that followed my previous coverage of Pizza Pizza Royalty Corp. (PZA:CA)(PZRIF). Its price took a nosedive after missing its Q3 2025 forecast but immediately rebounded as more buyers took a position. And I think that makes PZA more interesting, supported by its high-margin, asset-light business model. Valuation is still attractive even after breaking above its previous high. Technicals are still bullish but warrant some caution due to recent overbuying.

Projection/Valuation

As we approach the earnings release season, you may be wondering how Pizza Pizza Royalty Corp. performed for the whole year. In line with this, I made my own estimation to have a glimpse of its potential results. That way, we can also have an estimate of its valuation.

I started with its revenue or royalty income by assuming a growth rate of 2.0%. I think that’s logical if we relate it to the revenue trend for the three quarters of 2025. They averaged at 2.0% (Q1 at 1.04%, Q2 at 3.0%, Q3 at 2.0%). Using this, I derived my estimated revenue of CAD $40.596M. This is slightly lower than the TTM revenue of CAD $40.6M. So, I believe my estimation is still conservative and realistic. For 2026-2029, I made an incremental revenue increase from 3.0% to 5.0%.

Meanwhile, I got the operating expenses by setting them as a percentage of revenue. Based on the 2020-2024 average, operating expenses comprised 1.73% of revenues. But to be conservative, I set it at 2.0%. I also set the operating assets and liabilities as a percentage of revenue to get the working capital. After adjusting it to NOPAT, I was also able to derive FCFF or free cash flow to the firm.

Using these numbers, I also assessed PZA’s valuation using the DCF Model. As you can see, I set its perpetual growth rate at 2.00% to be in line with my revenue assumption for 2025 and the target inflation by the Fed. Meanwhile, I estimated the WACC of 6.80% using the capital asset pricing model. That way, I could estimate the EV or enterprise value of the stock. Lastly, I adjusted the most recent cash and debt to get the equity value before dividing it by the common shares outstanding. With that, my TP or target price, is CAD $19.27. If I add a 5% risk consideration, my TP will be $18.31. This means that PZA is still reasonably valued at this level. Also, the TP suggests 15-20% upside potential.

Another forward-looking method is the Dividend Discount Model. Its annualized dividend is $0.93 based on the monthly payout of $0.0775. Its dividend growth rate is 6.65% based on the five-year CAGR reported on Seeking Alpha. But to be more conservative, I set its risk premium to 8.0%. Lastly, I set its levered Beta at 1.00, assuming PZA to be in line with the S&P 500. Using all these numbers, my new TP is $20.26. If I add a 10% risk consideration, it will be $18.24. This is in line with my DCF TP. Hence, we may verify that PZA is still cheap today.

Risks

PZA must watch out for stubborn inflation in the US and Canada. I understand that its business model is not cost-sensitive since it only owns royalties or trademark rights for Pizza Pizza and Pizza 73. It neither owns nor franchises restaurants. It only collects royalty fees or a percentage of system sales of these brands. However, inflation may have an indirect impact on its royalty income. Stubborn inflation may affect purchasing power and discretionary spending. So, foot traffic in these restaurants may also be affected. Softer demand and sales mean lower royalty income. In the US, inflation has already slowed down to 2.7% but remains close to the 14-month high. Canada’s inflation is quite tamer at 2.4%, but positive inflation means higher prices on a YoY basis.

PZA (Pizza Pizza and Pizza 73) must also beware of the tight competition. Domino’s Pizza (DPZ) continues to dominate the QSR pizza market with its successful capitalization on technologies and stronger delivery arm. Other large and popular competitors like Papa John’s (PZZA), Boston Pizza (BPZZF), and Pizza Hut (YUM) are also there. Note that Domino’s has 638 locations in Canada, Pizza Hut has 620, while Boston Pizza has 374.

Mitigants/Drivers

Despite these challenges, I believe that PZA is still well-positioned.

One of the things I like about it is its high-margin, asset-light business model. It’s true that inflation can affect its royalties. However, it does not necessarily affect its operating expenses since PZA only cares about acquiring trademark rights of restaurants to be added to its royalty pool. So it does not have to worry about the cost of raw materials and ingredients, delivery expenses, and investment in new technologies. That is why it doesn’t have depreciation and capex. It can just relax while the actual restaurant owners and franchisees work hard to improve or sustain their sales.

Moreover, Pizza Pizza remains a strong brand with 694 locations compared to 672 in 2024. Its system sales reached CAD137.997M , up by 2.3% YoY from CAD $134.924M. This completely offset the slight weakness in Pizza 73 stores in the royalty pool. So, the total system sales still increased by 2.0%.

In the same way, PZA enjoyed higher royalty income of CAD $10.159M, up by 1.9% YoY from CAD $9.971M. This was also the third quarter that PZA posted positive royalty revenue growth. Meanwhile, the operating expenses are very low relative to revenues. So, the operating margin remains very high at 98.0%.

Another factor to note is its robust liquidity. PZA’s low capital intensity assures us of its sustainable operations and dividends. Cash and cash equivalents are low at CAD $4.0M since PZA uses the majority of its earnings to pay dividends. Meanwhile, its borrowings are high, but the maturity profile is decent because no amount will mature within the year. This is in line with the Net Debt/EBITA of 1.08x. This means that PZA only needs a year to repay the whole amount even if it doesn’t have to. Of course, the company doesn’t have to deplete EBITA since borrowings will not mature all at once. Suppose PZA only uses 80% of EBITA or CAD $31.8M (CAD $39.7M * 0.80). The ratio will increase to 1.3x. But just the same, PZA only needs about a year to repay the whole amount at once. This proves that it has adequate reserves to sustain its capacity and dividends.

Lastly, PZA may benefit from the recent policy rate cut by the Fed and BoC. In only three months, the interest rates in the US and Canada have already been slashed to 75 bps and 50 bps, respectively. This can boost economic activity and help improve employment and wages. Also, this makes credit cards more accessible and affordable, which can support discretionary spending. So, consumers may become more capable of spending on fast-food chains. PZA may indirectly benefit from it since more sales means more royalties. More importantly, lower interest rates mean lower repricing rates of its borrowings with variable rates, which can help improve its profitability and liquidity.

Technicals

PZA’s technicals are bullish again as the price continues to rebound from the sharp dip in Q4 2025. The price increases with higher highs and lows as it approaches the CAD $16.44 resistance. It also trends above the 50-day and 200-day SMA, which means that the current momentum is rebounding and stronger than the average in the past 50 and 200 days. In addition, the 50-day SMA is starting to shift its direction, which suggests that recent market sentiments have become more optimistic.

Its MACD and RSI show the same thing but warrant some caution. The MACD line is increasing above the signal line, which shows increasing buying volume. The histogram is within positive territory, which implies that buying volume still outpaces selling volume. However, we should note that histogram is getting close to zero or neutral zone because selling volume is also starting to increase. This can be explained by its RSI. The stock is already overbought, so profit-takers are acting on it, which may lead to a dip. Also, the market may start to become cautious ahead of the Q4 2025 release. On a lighter note, the dip may give us new buying opportunities at a discount.

Conclusion

Pizza Pizza Royalty Corp. remains very attractive with its cheap valuation and upside potential. This can be justified by its robust fundamentals and strategic business model. Its dividends are also high for the stock price and remain attractive and sustainable. Technicals adhere with bullish signals but warrant some caution due to overbuying. I’m reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Nebius Is My Number One Stock For 2026, But Not For The Reason You Think
data.description:
Nebius (NBIS) is one of my favorite stocks on the market.

The company - in case you're not aware - is a pure-play GPU cloud provider. It plans to generate substantial revenue growth, EBITDA margins, and economic returns to shareholders in the coming years.

In previous coverage here on Seeking Alpha, I have discussed the company's prospects, highlighting its relatively attractive valuation, significant scaling potential, and strong unit economics that ultimately make up my investment thesis.

However, one thing I have not yet covered is an emerging trend that I believe will reshape parts of the software industry: the impact of AI tools on traditional "off-the-rack" software businesses.

Demand for AI is obviously a core part of the thesis for Nebius - but I also believe the rapid progression of tools like Claude Code, Cursor, and other AI-powered development platforms is turning many non-AI software applications into increasingly commoditized products.

The stock market has already begun to recognize this dynamic to some extent, with shares of ServiceNow (NOW), Docusign (DOCU), Atlassian (TEAM), and others down meaningfully over the past year:

That said, I don't believe that NBIS - a direct beneficiary of this shift - has yet seen a corresponding bump in premium.

As a result, today, I will reiterate my thesis for the company, dive deeper into this emerging software commoditization trend, and make the case that shares of Nebius will move significantly higher in 2026.

Sound good? Let's dive into my number one pick for the next year.

The Thesis

Before digging further into this software shift / tailwind, it is worth outlining how I believe the market currently views Nebius today.

In short, the company is a pure-play GPU cloud provider set to scale revenues dramatically as demand from AI models grows substantially in the coming years.

Unlike many other providers, Nebius has built out a fully verticalized GPU cloud stack, making it easy for developers of all stripes to access AI-optimized compute in the modern digital age.

As one of the few companies with preferred access to industry-leading Nvidia chips and rising investor interest, analysts are currently projecting significant multi-year growth for the business.

Over the past twelve months, Nebius generated approximately $363 million in revenue.

Looking ahead, analysts currently expect revenue growth of roughly 373% in 2025, 521% in 2026, 125% in 2027, and 83% in 2028. Taken together, this implies that over the next 36 months, Nebius could scale revenue to more than $14 billion:

This is one of the most exponential growth curves you can find on the market today.

Additionally, the combination of limited compute supply, a strong full-stack GPU offering, and a favorable pricing environment support this bullish outlook.

On the earnings side, analysts expect continued losses through 2025 and 2026, with the company becoming profitable around 2027. That timeline makes sense to me, as near-term infrastructure spending is likely to outpace short-term revenue growth, as the business 'stands up' the revenue generating assets:

The good news is that the long-term opportunity here is substantial, given the attractive unit economics of GPU cloud businesses. As I recently discussed in my coverage of CoreWeave, GPU cloud economics are particularly compelling, with very high gross margins and significant operating leverage over time:

On a 'real' profitability level, CRWV recently produced a net income of -$110 million. However, when you add back in depreciation and amortization, along with stock-based compensation, you get a positive 'operating' cash flow of roughly $660 million. We put 'operating' in quotes here because we're not including the chunky payables numbers that skew regular OCF considerably; rather, with this number, we believe we're getting at how profitable the company actually is when you strip out recurring non-cash items.

On a revenue base of $1.37 billion, this implies a strong operating margin of nearly 50%.

Nebius is in a similar position, with reported gross margins in the 70 percent plus range. While depreciation and amortization will meaningfully dampen near-term, per-chip profitability, I still expect operating margins to eventually reach 30 percent or more, inclusive of depreciation costs.

Top-tier cloud businesses such as Amazon Web Services have proven capable of generating operating margins above 35%, and I see no reason why a focused, pure-play GPU cloud provider could not achieve similar margins over time:

Perhaps doubly so, given NBIS's focus on in-demand GPU compute capacity.

From a valuation perspective, analyst estimates imply a roughly 3.5x FY2 price-to-sales multiple, and a FY2 price-to-earnings multiple of approximately 46x:

While 46x earnings is expensive relative to the broader market, the company's growth trajectory implies that these multiples should compress rapidly as revenue scales into the end of the decade. Additionally, GPU cloud revenues tend to be highly recurring, creating a sticky customer base and strong operating leverage.

Taken together, these valuation metrics look attractive relative to other high-growth opportunities.

Since my last coverage in September, the company has delivered another strong quarterly earnings report. Nebius reported roughly 40 percent quarter-over-quarter sales growth and announced a new contract with Meta:

[A]bout the demand. Q3 demand was very strong. We sold out all of our available capacity. We continue to see a consistent trend. Every time we bring capacity online, we sold all of it.

Today, we are very pleased to announce that we signed another major deal at this time with Meta for approximately $3 billion over the next 5 years. In fact, the demand for this capacity was overwhelming and the size of the contract was limited to the amount of capacity that we had available, which means that if we had more, we could have sold more.

...

Q3 group revenue was $146 million, up nearly 355% year-over-year and 39% quarter-over-quarter. Annualized run rate revenue for the core business at the end of September was $551 million... I'm also pleased to say that adjusted EBITDA margin for the core infrastructure business expanded quarter-over-quarter to nearly 19%.

This is clearly a demand-constrained business that is scaling rapidly and executing on its data center expansion plans.

Between the valuation, the economics, and the earnings trajectory, I view Nebius as a Strong Buy.

Additional Tailwinds

At a baseline, Nebius already stands out as one of the best growth opportunities in the market.

However, in recent months, I have been thinking more deeply about the broader impact AI will have on software businesses.

AI has obvious implications for content creation, legal services, design, engineering, robotics, and more. But one of the largest impacts may ultimately be on software engineering itself. We are already seeing early signs of this in a weaker hiring market for new software engineering graduates:

Historically, software companies have built efficient tech systems designed to solve specific business problems, such as collaboration, task management, time tracking, workflow automation, resource planning, and more.

It has historically made sense for companies to outsource these capabilities (through recurring software subscriptions), in order to preserve focus on revenue-generating activities.

Today, however, things are different. Tools like Claude Code and Cursor dramatically reduce the cost and complexity of building internal software, which means that small teams can now replicate or replace many non-AI SaaS products, especially inside medium and large enterprises that have the resources to deploy.

Why pay for an expensive subscription when an internal tool can be built at a fraction of the cost and tailored precisely to internal needs?

As a result, I believe economic value will shift away from traditional SaaS and towards AI-driven workloads.

The impact on NBIS here is straightforward. AI-native applications will matter, but they will also require persistent GPU access and scalable infrastructure. That is exactly where NBIS sits in the value chain. At this layer, economics are more durable and demand is more structural. I do not believe the market has fully priced in this reality. Plus, with recent developments in NBIS's Token Factory Inference engine, it's easy to draw a straight line between the dominance of AI over traditional software and NBIS's ultimate economics:

To extend our addressable market opportunity to large enterprise customers, we released our new enterprise-ready cloud platform version 3.0 called Aether and our new inference platform called Nebius Token Factory. We believe Aether gives organizations the trust, control and simplicity they need to run their most critical AI workloads. Nebius Token Factory is a production scale inference platform that enables organizations to run open source models with reliability, visibility and control.

Based on the strength in demand that we see and our accelerated capacity growth plan, we believe we can achieve annualized run rate revenue ARR of $7 billion to $9 billion by the end of 2026.

As value shifts down the stack toward AI models and the infrastructure that supports them, Nebius is positioned to capture an outsized share of long-term returns.

This dynamic has begun to show up in traditional software stocks that have traded lower in recent months, but I do not believe the scale or speed of this shift has been reflected in NBIS's valuation, which could soon reflect a mid-single-digit sales multiple. I believe a low teens sales multiple would make more sense given the growth opportunity, margins, and durability of future cash flows.

This incremental tailwind is what upgrades Nebius from a top growth idea to my favorite stock for 2026.

Risks

There are, of course, meaningful risks associated with investing in Nebius, as there are with most high-growth companies.

If revenue growth fails to meet expectations, then the multiple investors are willing to pay for the business would likely compress. While I believe shares are attractively priced today, valuation multiples can always move lower.

Execution is another key risk. Standing up data centers is a capital-intensive and technically complex process involving construction, engineering, and operations. Management has executed well so far, which gives me a level of confidence, but this remains a core risk.

Finally, higher interest rates would likely pressure the stock. As a high-growth company with much of its current value tied to future cash flows, Nebius is particularly sensitive to changes in discount rates - perhaps more than any other company, given the growth trajectory.

Summary

Overall, though, Nebius is my favorite stock for 2026.

The company's growth profile, execution, and demand environment position it for a compelling revenue trajectory, and the stock remains attractively priced relative to that opportunity.

As value flows down the stack from traditional software toward AI models and the infrastructure that powers them, Nebius stands to benefit even more than previously anticipated. I believe NBIS should be worth a 10x+ sales multiple by the end of 2027, which implies incredible upside for current investors.

Thus, I am reiterating my Strong Buy rating.

Cheers.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Top 2026 Long Idea investment competition, which runs through February 28. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: GE Aerospace: Why A 52x P/E Is Not Extreme (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
GE Aerospace (GE) has been one of my favorite aerospace stocks. However, in October I downgraded the stock from buy to hold as the company’s stock price showed overvaluation against expected earnings in 2027, limiting strong market-outperforming stock price movements. Upon review of the valuation metrics, I am upgrading the stock back to buy.

Additionally, I will reflect on some comments I have received about GE Aerospace being expensive from a P/E perspective. That is a comment I get on quite a few other names as well, such as Bombardier and Rheinmetall AG. Those names actually are among my best-performing picks. In this report, I want to explain why P/E, in my view, is too limited to detect value timely. Additionally, I will discuss GE’s upcoming earnings.

Why 52x P/E is Deceiving in Simplicity

P/E ratios are widely used to assess whether a stock is cheap or not. I believe that is due to the apparent simplicity of the metric, which simply is the company’s share price divided by the earnings per share over the past twelve months or the next twelve months. We note that by the simplicity of the metric, it only takes equity and earnings after interest in mind. That is actually already where true value detection becomes problematic, as it does not fully take into account capital structure, which includes debt, cash, non-controlling interest, and preferred equity. Especially since P/E does not include any information on cash and debt balances, I believe it already is not a proper metric to benchmark stocks or sectors. Within a sector, there are a whole lot of different companies in the sense that each company has a specific capital structure driven by the maturity phase of the company or the product the company produces or the services rendered.

If you look at how GE Aerospace stacks against the market and the S&P 500, we see that using twelve-month trailing earnings, the stock is 14% cheaper than the market and 43% cheaper than the aerospace and defense peer group. On forward metrics, GE Aerospace indeed appears to be expensive, trading almost 70% above the market and 14% above the peer group. On a forward basis, GE Aerospace and the aerospace and defense industry both are expensive, and I believe that is driven by the nature of the business with long-life programs with more cash flow visibility compared to the market, and GE’s business with a long-term tail in aftermarket sales on engines should have even better visibility, and that explains the “expensive” nature of GE’s multiple.

As mentioned, P/E does not capture the capital structure of a business, and as a result, it also does not account for cash flows and debt repayments. The metric that allows for that is enterprise value. It allows projections of cash and debt balances. Cash is not part of the capital structure, but rather it offsets debt and therefore is indirectly part of the capital structure. The ability to project these balance sheet items allows for a more meaningful projection of price targets and makes comparisons between the valuation of companies as it adjusts for the capital structure of a company. That is why I use EV/EBITDA as an anchor point to set price targets. It is a valuation method that captures more than just share price and net income. It captures balance sheet quality and operating performance against the stock price.

GE Aerospace Stock Upgrade to Buy

It is not to say that GE Aerospace stock is looking more attractive based on EV/EBITDA, because the company currently trades at 33.9x EV/EBITDA, while the aerospace and defense industry trades at 24.4x, providing an even higher premium. However, at this point the stock price is also highly likely to reflect forward earnings for 2027, which is interesting to assess using our valuation principles.

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

When we look at GE Aerospace’s valuation, we see that the stock is currently fairly valued with 2027 earnings in mind using the EV/EBITDA for the peer group. That makes sense, as I believe the market should be forward-looking. Previously, I used the company’s longer-term EV/EBITDA to determine price targets for the company median-driven price target. In this valuation update, I have corrected that. It makes little sense to value the stock like that conglomerate that it no longer is. Therefore, I have used the EV/EBITDA median over the past three years, which shows an 11% upside and a $346.31 price target and justifies a buy rating. We note that the median EV/EBITDA exceeds the trading levels of peers. To some, that may seem to imply overvaluation. However, we do note that the company has a business setup that aligns it for long-term cash flow generation from each engine it delivers and that, in my view, warrants a premium valuation.

Expected EBITDA increased by 2.3% for 2025-2027 with a 1.9% lift to estimates for 2025, 1.6% for 2026, and 3.2% for 2027. Free cash flow estimates improved a bit less, as higher capital expenditures offset higher operating cash flow expectations.

For GE Aerospace, we see that margins are expected to improve incrementally but not substantially in the years ahead. I do expect the margins to eventually hit at least 27%-29%. Cash flow performance is expected to show consistent growth, with around 70% cash conversion resulting in capital expenditures to account for 14% of operating cash flow. We note that the cash balance is set to gradually drop, but that is not a reason for concern when keeping the net-debt leverage in mind. At 0.43x by 2027, it actually offers the space to increase share repurchases by around $3.4-$3.5 billion more than currently modeled. The reason I have not modeled it at this moment is because it does not materially add upside in the near term.

What Are Analysts Expecting for GE Aerospace in Q4 2025?

For the fourth quarter, analysts are expecting GE Aerospace to report $10.48-$11.57 billion in sales, with an $11.21 billion consensus. This marks 13.4% annual growth. Over the past months, analyst estimates have been adjusted up by 2% for the fourth quarter. On EPS, analysts expect 8.7% growth with a consensus estimate of $1.43, with a low estimate of $1.34 and a high estimate of $1.51. Over the past six months, estimates have been revised up by 6.6%.

Conclusion: GE Aerospace is a Buy After Re-Alignment of Valuation Metrics

I previously downgraded GE Aerospace stock to hold as the company showed no upside against the peer group EV/EBITDA or the company median multiple. However, the multiple that I used reflected the conglomerate General Electric and not GE Aerospace. In my update I have corrected that, and we see around 11% upside. I am typically looking for roughly 11% upside, and that is why I am upgrading the stock back to buy.
